mnemonics.title: UPER Substitution
mnemonics.content: Unification = Pattern Matching - Replace variables systematically. Remember: Unified terms must have same function symbols, arity, and structure after substitution.

mnemonics.title: MGU - Most General Unifier
mnemonics.content: MGU: 'Most General Unifier' - It's the substitution that does the LEAST specific work, keeping maximum flexibility for future推理 (reasoning). Less restrictive = more general.

mnemonics.title: Occurs Check
mnemonics.content: Never let X = f(X) - This creates infinite terms! The occurs check says: 'If variable appears in its own definition, STOP the unification - it's impossible!'

mnemonics.title: Resolution Rule
mnemonics.content: Resolution: (P ∨ Q) ∧ (¬P ∨ R) ⇒ (Q ∨ R). Remember: 'Cancel complementary literals, combine the rest' - like positive meets negative and they annihilate!

mnemonics.title: Substitution Composition
mnemonics.content: Substitutions compose: {X/Y} ○ {Y/Z} = {X/Z, Y/Z}. Order matters! Apply right substitution first, then left. Think: 'Right to left in composition'.

acronyms.usage: Use when you need the least restrictive substitution that still makes two terms identical. It's unique if it exists.

acronyms.expansion: UNIfy - Make terms Y equal by finding substitutions
acronyms.usage: Use when solving unification problems: check structure, collect equations, apply substitutions.
Use the resolution inference rule: find one positive and one negative literal of the same predicate, cancel them, OR the remaining clauses.
Use notation like {X/f(a), Y/b} to show what variables get replaced by which terms.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine two jigsaw puzzle pieces - unification finds if they fit together and how. Variables are empty slots, constants are fixed shapes. Only matching shapes (same function symbols) can unite!
Think of MGU as the 'widest net' that catches the most possibilities. It makes terms equal but keeps variables as flexible as possible - like a loose-fit glove vs. a tight custom glove.

Picture two people arguing: Person A says 'It is raining (P)' OR 'I'll go out (Q)'. Person B says 'It is NOT raining (¬P)' OR 'I'll stay in (R)'. They agree on P=not P, so they cancel out, leaving 'I'll go out OR I'll stay in (Q ∨ R)'.
Visualize infinite recursion: X = f(X) means X = f(f(f(...))) - an infinite tree that never terminates. The occurs check is the 'stop' sign that prevents building infinite structures.
Think of substitutions as 'layers' of replacement. {X/Y}○{Y/Z} means: first replace Y with Z, then replace X (now pointing to Y) with Z. Like dominoes falling - follow the chain!

analogies.analogy: Unification is like finding the 'common denominator' in algebraic expressions - you find what values make two expressions mathematically equivalent. Just as 2x and 2y become equal when x=y, predicate terms become identical through substitutions.

Resolution is like 'proof by contradiction' in debates. If you assume the opposite of what you want to prove and show it leads to a contradiction (P and NOT P), you've proven your original statement. The resolution rule automates this 'reductio ad absurdum' process.

MGU is like a universal key that opens all locks it can, but no more than necessary. A specific substitution like {X/cat} opens only 'cat' doors, while MGU {X/Y} keeps Y flexible to become anything later.
Substitution is like pronoun replacement in language. 'John loves John' becomes 'He loves himself' with substitution {He/John, himself/John}. Variables are pronouns waiting to be assigned meaning.
Resolving two clauses is like 'cancelling' in fractions. Just as +5 and -5 cancel to 0, literal P and ¬P cancel to nothing. What's left combines (ORs together) to form the new derived clause.
